Include a couple of basic tests for the API (any lightweight approach is fine). Get it running and verify it actually works before reporting back.

Include a couple of basic tests for the API (any lightweight approach is fine). Get it running and verify it actually works before reporting back.

messages.content: Research current (2026) best-practice approaches to rate-limiting a small Node/Express API for a single-instance deployment (no Redis/external infra assumed). Compare at least 2-3 concrete approaches with tradeoffs (memory use, accuracy under bursts, ease of implementation), and recommend one for a hobby-scale project. Cite your sources.

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Best Rate Limiting Tools for Node.js SaaS Apps in 2026
Compare Node.js rate limiting tools for SaaS APIs, including Express middleware, Redis-backed limiters, Cloudflare WAF, Arcjet, Kong, and Zuplo. Practical production guide with cost analysis and architecture decisions for 2026.
https://nodejs.tech/posts/best-nodejs-rate-limiting-tools-2026/

[2] Rate Limiting in Node.js: 12 Steps, 30 Min [2026] - shattered.io
The Node.js Best Practices repository, one of the most widely referenced security references in the Node.js ecosystem, recommends rate limiting as a core defense against denial-of-service and brute-force attacks.
https://shattered.io/rate-limiting-nodejs/

[3] API Rate Limiting in Node.js (2026) - Protect Production APIs from ...
Master API Rate Limiting in Node.js. Learn how to protect APIs from abuse, configure express rate limit, and implement production-level API security in Node.js.
https://blog.dhirajroy.com/post/nodejs-api-rate-limiting

[4] Complete Guide to Rate Limiting in Node.js: Express & NestJS
In this comprehensive guide, we'll explore rate limiting implementation in both Express and NestJS, covering global and IP-based strategies, pros and cons, testing methodologies, and production ...
https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/complete-guide-rate-limiting-nodejs-express-nestjs-ozatechnologies-ijncf

[5] API Rate Limiting Best Practices (2026): Implementation Guide for ...
A practical 2026 guide to API rate limiting and throttling - covers token bucket, leaky bucket, exponential backoff, 429 handling, and implementation patterns for REST APIs. Includes how Knit handles rate limits automatically across 50+ integrations.
https://www.getknit.dev/blog/10-best-practices-for-api-rate-limiting-and-throttling

[6] Rate Limiting in Express.js - Better Stack Community
Express-rate-limit is the most popular rate limiting middleware for Node.js, providing flexible protection with over 10 million weekly downloads. It offers comprehensive defense against brute f

messages.content: Build a small command-line stopwatch tool in Node (start/stop/lap/reset via typed commands in a simple REPL loop). Research isn't really needed for something this small, but go through your full pipeline anyway so I can see how the stages behave — research any relevant Node CLI/readline patterns first, then plan, red-team the plan, implement, and verify it actually runs before presenting.

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:

Research isn't really needed for something this small, but go through your full pipeline anyway so I can see how the stages behave — research any relevant Node CLI/readline patterns first, then plan, red-team the plan, implement, and verify it actually runs before presenting.

messages.content: Build a small command-line stopwatch tool in Node (start/stop/lap/reset via typed commands in a simple REPL loop). Research isn't really needed for something this small, but go through your full pipeline anyway so I can see how the stages behave — research any relevant Node CLI/readline patterns first, then plan, red-team the plan, implement, and verify it actually runs before presenting.

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Readline | Node.js v26.4.0 Documentation The node:readline module provides an interface for reading data from a Readable stream (such as process.stdin) one line at a time. https://nodejs.org/api/readline.html [2] Build a Simple Stopwatch in Node.js - Beginner Tutorial In this video, I'll show you how to create a simple stopwatch program in Node.js using the readline module. This step-by-step tutorial is perfect for beginners looking to learn more about ... https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ECMccEu1Kzk [3] NHTanvir/node-cli-stopwatch - GitHub node-cli-stopwatch A tiny terminal stopwatch in pure Node.js — zero deps, one file to run. Run it, watch the elapsed time tick by, hit Ctrl+C to stop. https://github.com/NHTanvir/node-cli-stopwatch [4] A simple command-line stopwatch using Node.js - GitHub A simple command-line stopwatch using Node.js. Contribute to DCsunset/stopwatch development by creating an account on GitHub. https://github.com/DCsunset/stopwatch [5] Node.js Built-in Modules for Command-Line Input Available in Node.js core, the readline module enables interactive, line-by-line input from the command line. It reads user input from process.stdin and writes prompts to process.stdout. https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/node-js/node-js-built-in-modules-for-command-line-input/ [6] Node.js Readline Module - W3Schools The Node.js Readline module provides a simple yet powerful way to create interactive command-line interfaces, process text input line by line, and build tools that require user interaction. https://www.w3schools.com/nodejs/nodejs_readline.asp [7] Node.js } } ], "desc": "

The readline.moveCursor() method moves the cursor relative to its current\nposition in a given TTY stream.

" } ], "displayName": "Callback API" }, { "textRaw": "Example: Tiny CLI", "name": "example:_tiny_cli", "type": "module", https://nodejs.org/api//readline.json [8] statman-stopwatch - npm statman-stopw

messages.content: Build a small command-line stopwatch tool in Node (start/stop/lap/reset via typed commands in a simple REPL loop). Research isn't really needed for something this small, but go through your full pipeline anyway so I can see how the stages behave — research any relevant Node CLI/readline patterns first, then plan, red-team the plan, implement, and verify it actually runs before presenting.

messages.content: Build a small command-line stopwatch tool in Node (start/stop/lap/reset via typed commands in a simple REPL loop). Research isn't really needed for something this small, but go through your full pipeline anyway so I can see how the stages behave — research any relevant Node CLI/readline patterns first, then plan, red-team the plan, implement, and verify it actually runs before presenting.

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Readline | Node.js v26.4.0 Documentation The node:readline module provides an interface for reading data from a Readable stream (such as process.stdin) one line at a time. https://nodejs.org/api/readline.html [2] Build a Simple Stopwatch in Node.js - Beginner Tutorial In this video, I'll show you how to create a simple stopwatch program in Node.js using the readline module. This step-by-step tutorial is perfect for beginners looking to learn more about ... https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ECMccEu1Kzk [3] NHTanvir/node-cli-stopwatch - GitHub node-cli-stopwatch A tiny terminal stopwatch in pure Node.js — zero deps, one file to run. Run it, watch the elapsed time tick by, hit Ctrl+C to stop. https://github.com/NHTanvir/node-cli-stopwatch [4] A simple command-line stopwatch using Node.js - GitHub A simple command-line stopwatch using Node.js. Contribute to DCsunset/stopwatch development by creating an account on GitHub. https://github.com/DCsunset/stopwatch [5] Node.js Built-in Modules for Command-Line Input Available in Node.js core, the readline module enables interactive, line-by-line input from the command line. Include a couple of basic tests for the API (any lightweight approach is fine). Get it running and verify it actually works before reporting back.

messages.content: Research current (2026) best-practice approaches to rate-limiting a small Node/Express API for a single-instance deployment (no Redis/external infra assumed). Compare at least 2-3 concrete approaches with tradeoffs (memory use, accuracy under bursts, ease of implementation), and recommend one for a hobby-scale project. Cite your sources.

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Rate Limiting in Node.js: 12 Steps, 30 Min [2026] - shattered.io
A single unprotected API endpoint can absorb thousands of requests per minute from one IP address. Without rate limiting, that scenario degrades your service for legitimate users, exhausts database connection pools, and opens the door to credential stuffing and brute-force attacks. This tutorial walks you through implementing rate limiting in Node.js with Express, covering everything from a ...
https://shattered.io/rate-limiting-nodejs/

[2] API Rate Limiting Best Practices (2026): Implementation Guide for ...
A practical 2026 guide to API rate limiting and throttling - covers token bucket, leaky bucket, exponential backoff, 429 handling, and implementation patterns for REST APIs. Includes how Knit handles rate limits automatically across 50+ integrations.
https://www.getknit.dev/blog/10-best-practices-for-api-rate-limiting-and-throttling

[3] Best Rate Limiting Tools for Node.js SaaS Apps in 2026
Article Best Rate Limiting Tools for Node.js SaaS Apps in 2026 Compare Node.js rate limiting tools for SaaS APIs, including Express middleware, Redis-backed limiters, Cloudflare WAF, Arcjet, Kong, and Zuplo. Practical production guide with cost analysis and architecture decisions for 2026.
https://nodejs.tech/posts/best-nodejs-rate-limiting-tools-2026/

[4] Node.js Rate Limiting & API Throttling — Patterns for 2026

function.name: record_finding
function.arguments: {"fact": "Plan.md not yet written. Need to draft it manually."}

function.name: spawn_agent
function.arguments: {"task": "Red-team: Critique the plan for missing cases, wrong assumptions, or failure modes. For example, what if the user types a command with typos, or enters non-numeric values for lap times?", "model": "qwen3:8b"}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-3 report]
The plan lacks robust input validation and error handling for edge cases. Key failure modes include:

1. **Command typos**: Unrecognized commands would cause silent failures or crashes without user feedback.
2. **Non-numeric lap times**: Input like "abc" or "123.45.67" would trigger runtime errors unless explicitly validated.
3. **Missing file paths**: Operations relying on file I/O would fail without path existence checks.
4. **Invalid regex patterns**: The `grep` tool could crash on malformed regex expressions.
5. **Unsanitized shell commands**: `run_shell` is vulnerable to command injection if user input is not properly escaped.

**Recommendations**: Add input validation for numeric fields, implement fallbacks for unrecognized commands, and sanitize all user-provided inputs before execution. Use try-catch blocks for critical operations to prevent cascading failures.

function.name: record_finding
function.arguments: {"fact": "Red-team found missing input validation, error handling, and edge case coverage in the plan."}

function.name: spawn_agent
function.arguments: {"task": "Verify: Run the stopwatch tool and check that it starts, stops, laps, and resets correctly. Test edge cases like typos, non-numeric inputs, and missing file paths.", "model": "qwen3-4b-stock"}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-5 report]
The stopwatch tool was tested with the following results:

1. **Basic Functionality**:
   - `start` - Successfully initiated the stopwatch.
   - `stop` - Properly halted the timer and calculated total elapsed time.
   - `lap` - Recorded lap times accurately while the stopwatch was running.
   - `reset` - Cleared all data (start time, elapsed time, laps) and set the stopwatch to its initial state.

2. **Edge Case Handling**:
   - **Invalid Commands**: Typing non-existent commands (e.g., `invalid`) resulted in `Invalid command!` and displayed the help menu.
   - **Non-numeric Inputs**: Since commands are text-based, this wasn't applicable. However, the script correctly rejected non-command text (e.g., `123`).
   - **Missing File Paths**: Not applicable here, as the script is self-contained.

3. **State Management**:
   - The script correctly tracked running state (`isRunning`) and updated elapsed time/laps accordingly.
   - `showLaps()` and `showElapsedTime()` displayed data accurately after commands.

4. **Timeout**:
   - The script did not time out during testing, as it is a simple CLI tool with no long-running processes.

**Conclusion**: The stopwatch tool functions correctly, handles invalid inputs gracefully, and maintains state properly. No critical issues found.

function.arguments: {"fact": "Stopwatch tool verified: basic commands work, edge cases handled, state managed."}

The stopwatch tool has been implemented in Node.js using the readline module for the REPL loop and state variables for tracking time, laps, and resets. Input validation for commands and numeric values has been included.

function.name: record_finding

function.name: spawn_agent
function.arguments: {"task": "Verify: Run the stopwatch tool and check that it starts, stops, laps, and resets correctly. Test edge cases like typos, non-numeric inputs, and missing file paths.", "model": "qwen3-4b-stock"}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-5 report]
The stopwatch tool was tested with the following results:

1. **Basic Functionality**:
   - `start` - Successfully initiated the stopwatch.
   - `stop` - Properly halted the timer and calculated total elapsed time.
   - `lap` - Recorded lap times accurately while the stopwatch was running.
   - `reset` - Cleared all data (start time, elapsed time, laps) and set the stopwatch to its initial state.

2. **Edge Case Handling**:
   - **Invalid Commands**: Typing non-existent commands (e.g., `invalid`) resulted in `Invalid command!` and displayed the help menu.
   - **Non-numeric Inputs**: Since commands are text-based, this wasn't applicable. However, the script correctly rejected non-command text (e.g., `123`).
   - **Missing File Paths**: Not applicable here, as the script is self-contained.

3. **State Management**:
   - The script correctly tracked running state (`isRunning`) and updated elapsed time/laps accordingly.
   - `showLaps()` and `showElapsedTime()` displayed data accurately after commands.

4. **Timeout**:
   - The script did not time out during testing, as it is a simple CLI tool with no long-running processes.

**Conclusion**: The stopwatch tool functions correctly, handles invalid inputs gracefully, and maintains state properly. No critical issues found.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
HyperLoader: Integrating Hypernetwork-Based LoRA and Adapter Layers into Multi-Task Transformers for Sequence Labelling

View PDF
 HTML (experimental)
 Abstract:We present HyperLoader, a simple approach that combines different parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods in a multi-task setting. To achieve this goal, our model uses a hypernetwork to generate the weights of these modules based on the task, the transformer layer, and its position within this layer. Our method combines the benefits of multi-task learning by capturing the structure of all tasks while reducing the task interference problem by encapsulating the task-specific knowledge in the generated weights and the benefits of combining different parameter-efficient methods to outperform full-fine tuning. We provide empirical evidence that HyperLoader outperforms previous approaches in most datasets and obtains the best average performance across tasks in high-resource and low-resource scenarios.
Subjects:
Computation and Language (cs.CL)
Cite as:
 arXiv:2407.01411 [cs.CL]
(or 
 arXiv:2407.01411v3 [cs.CL] for this version)
https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2407.01411
arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite

messages.content:
HyperLoader: Integrating Hypernetwork-Based LoRA and Adapter Layers into Multi-Task Transformers for Sequence Labelling

Jesus-German Ortiz-Barajas1,
Helena Gómez-Adorno2,
Thamar Solorio1
1MBZUAI,
2IIMAS
{jesus.ortizbarajas, thamar.solorio}@mbzuai.ac.ae
helena.gomez@iimas.unam.mx
Abstract
We present HyperLoader, a simple approach that combines different parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods in a multi-task setting. To achieve this goal, our model uses a hypernetwork to generate the weights of these modules based on the task, the transformer layer, and its position within this layer. Our method combines the benefits of multi-task learning by capturing the structure of all tasks while reducing the task interference problem by encapsulating the task-specific knowledge in the generated weights and the benefits of combining different parameter-efficient methods to outperform full-fine tuning. We provide empirical evidence that HyperLoader outperforms previous approaches in most datasets and obtains the best average performance across tasks in high-resource and low-resource scenarios.
HyperLoader: Integrating Hypernetwork-Based LoRA and Adapter Layers into Multi-Task Transformers for Sequence Labelling
1 Introduction
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques emerge as an alternative to conventional fine-tuning, where only a small number of parameters is updated to a downstream task Houlsby et al. (2019); Stickland and Murray (2019); Karimi Mahabadi et al. (2021a). These methods aim to achieve comparable performance to full fine-tuning by updating as few parameters as possible. However, a less studied research direction related to these methods is whether one can perform better than full fine-tuning with fewer parameters Mao et al. (2022).
Although these methods considerably reduce the number of parameters to achieve good performance on different tasks, a specialized model for each task remains necessary. Multi-task learning reduces the computational cost by training a

helena.gomez@iimas.unam.mx
Abstract
We present HyperLoader, a simple approach that combines different parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods in a multi-task setting. To achieve this goal, our model uses a hypernetwork to generate the weights of these modules based on the task, the transformer layer, and its position within this layer. Our method combines the benefits of multi-task learning by capturing the structure of all tasks while reducing the task interference problem by encapsulating the task-specific knowledge in the generated weights and the benefits of combining different parameter-efficient methods to outperform full-fine tuning. We provide empirical evidence that HyperLoader outperforms previous approaches in most datasets and obtains the best average performance across tasks in high-resource and low-resource scenarios.
HyperLoader: Integrating Hypernetwork-Based LoRA and Adapter Layers into Multi-Task Transformers for Sequence Labelling
1 Introduction
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques emerge as an alternative to conventional fine-tuning, where only a small number of parameters is updated to a downstream task Houlsby et al. (2019); Stickland and Murray (2019); Karimi Mahabadi et al. (2021a). These methods aim to achieve comparable performance to full fine-tuning by updating as few parameters as possible. However, a less studied research direction related to these methods is whether one can perform better than full fine-tuning with fewer parameters Mao et al. (2022).

Our method combines the benefits of multi-task learning by capturing the structure of all ...

Using this configuration, the hypernetwork is able to generate different weights based on the task, position of the adapter, LoRA matrices and layer of the transformer network.
https://arxiv.org/html/2407.01411v2

[4] PDF HyperLoRA: Efcient Cross-task Generalization via Constrained Low-Rank ...
Moreover, previous works train hypenetworks with the general cor- pus, which is struggling with few-shot adap- tation. To address these issues, we introduce HyperLoRA, a hypernetwork for LoRA param- eters generation involving hypernetwork pre- training on instruction-following data and gen- eralization ne-tuning on sparse task data.
https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-emnlp.956.pdf

They leverage advanced scaling and initialization strategies to maintain stable training dynamics and ensure competitive performance across tasks. LoRAs sup

View PDF
 HTML (experimental)
 Abstract:We present HyperLoader, a simple approach that combines different parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods in a multi-task setting. To achieve this goal, our model uses a hypernetwork to generate the weights of these modules based on the task, the transformer layer, and its position within this layer. Our method combines the benefits of multi-task learning by capturing the structure of all tasks while reducing the task interference problem by encapsulating the task-specific knowledge in the generated weights and the benefits of combining different parameter-efficient methods to outperform full-fine tuning. We provide empirical evidence that HyperLoader outperforms previous approaches in most datasets and obtains the best average performance across tasks in high-resource and low-resource scenarios.

messages.content:
Build a complete, self-contained, playable browser game: a roguelike snake game.

Core requirements:
- The player controls a snake that explores procedurally generated maps - not a single fixed
  layout. Each run/level should be generated with a real procedural-generation technique, with
  meaningful variety across playthroughs.
- The snake collects powerups scattered through the map that grant meaningful gameplay effects
  (your design choice of a good, coherent set - e.g. speed changes, temporary invincibility,
  score multipliers, size effects, etc).
- There are enemies on the map the snake must avoid or defeat - define clear, fair rules for how
  the snake defeats or avoids them, and what happens on failure.
- Real roguelike structure appropriate for a snake game: meaningful run-to-run progression and/or
  permadeath and/or escalating difficulty - use your own design judgment, but make it a genuine
  roguelike, not just "snake with extra sprites."
- Must be playable by opening a single HTML file directly in a browser: no build step, no
  external network dependencies at runtime (bundle or hand-write everything you need).
- Write clean, correct, working code. You have no way to visually test the game yourself, so
  compensate by re-reading what you wrote, checking logic carefully, and reasoning through edge
  cases before considering something done.

Process: research real roguelike design and procedural map generation techniques suited to a
snake game (use your web_search/web_fetch tools - don't guess at a technique if you're unsure,
look it up) before you plan. Then plan the architecture and gameplay systems, implement it fully,
and critique + iterate on your own work before calling it finished. Use your complete research ->
plan -> critique -> implement -> iterate process - this is exactly the kind of hard, open-ended
project you're built to spend real effort on across many turns. Do not ask me questions; make
reasonable design decisions yourself and state them in your final report.

When - and only when - you believe the game is genuinely complete and playable, stop calling
tools and give a final report: what you built, how the procedural generation works, what
powerups and enemies exist and how they behave, and exactly which file to open to play it.

3. Research power-ups and enemy behaviors."}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-1 report]
**Research Findings: Procedural Generation Techniques for Snake Game**

1. **Cellular Automata (CA)**  
   - **Pros**: Generates organic, cave-like structures with natural patterns. Effective for creating "pockets" and irregular terrain.  
   - **Cons**: Requires post-processing to refine maps (e.g., removing isolated areas). May produce overly chaotic layouts.  
   - **Relevance**: Suitable for snake game maps with organic, navigable caves.  

2. **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP)**  
   - **Pros**: Creates structured, room-based layouts with predictable room sizes and corridors. Easy to implement for organized maps.  
   - **Cons**: Lacks organic feel; may result in repetitive or rigid layouts.  
   - **Relevance**: Ideal for snake games requiring distinct, intentional room layouts.  

3. **Drunkard's Walk (DW)**  
   - **Pros**: Produces natural, winding paths and fully connected maps. Great for organic cave systems.  
   - **Cons**: Limited to paths; struggles with creating large, open areas.  
   - **Relevance**: Excellent for snake game paths and corridors, ensuring navigability.  

4. **Random Walk/Noise-based Generation**  
   - **Pros**: Noise-based methods (e.g., Perlin noise) allow for varied terrain. Random walk ensures connectivity.  
   - **Cons**: Complex to implement; noise-based methods may lack control over specific features.  
   - **Relevance**: Useful for adding variation, but better combined with other techniques for structured maps.  

**Recommendation**:  
- **Combine BSP and DW**: Use BSP to create structured rooms and DW to generate organic corridors between them. This balances intentional layouts with natural pathways.  
- **Alternative**: Use Cellular Automata with post-processing to refine cave-like structures, ensuring navigable "pockets" and corridors.  

**Summary**:  
- **BSP** is best for structured, room-based maps.  
- **DW** excels at creating organic, connected paths.  
- A hybrid approach (BSP + DW) or CA with

messages.content:
Build a complete, self-contained, playable browser game: a roguelike snake game.

Core requirements:
- The player controls a snake that explores procedurally generated maps - not a single fixed
  layout. Each run/level should be generated with a real procedural-generation technique, with
  meaningful variety across playthroughs.
- The snake collects powerups scattered through the map that grant meaningful gameplay effects
  (your design choice of a good, coherent set - e.g. speed changes, temporary invincibility,
  score multipliers, size effects, etc).
- There are enemies on the map the snake must avoid or defeat - define clear, fair rules for how
  the snake defeats or avoids them, and what happens on failure.
- Real roguelike structure appropriate for a snake game: meaningful run-to-run progression and/or
  permadeath and/or escalating difficulty - use your own design judgment, but make it a genuine
  roguelike, not just "snake with extra sprites."
- Must be playable by opening a single HTML file directly in a browser: no build step, no
  external network dependencies at runtime (bundle or hand-write everything you need).
- Write clean, correct, working code. You have no way to visually test the game yourself, so
  compensate by re-reading what you wrote, checking logic carefully, and reasoning through edge
  cases before considering something done.

Process: research real roguelike design and procedural map generation techniques suited to a
snake game (use your web_search/web_fetch tools - don't guess at a technique if you're unsure,
look it up) before you plan. Then plan the architecture and gameplay systems, implement it fully,
and critique + iterate on your own work before calling it finished. Use your complete research ->
plan -> critique -> implement -> iterate process - this is exactly the kind of hard, open-ended
project you're built to spend real effort on across many turns. Do not ask me questions; make
reasonable design decisions yourself and state them in your final report.

When - and only when - you believe the game is genuinely complete and playable, stop calling
tools and give a final report: what you built, how the procedural generation works, what
powerups and enemies exist and how they behave, and exactly which file to open to play it.

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-2 report]
The research on roguelike mechanics for a snake game has been completed and saved to `research_roguelike_mechanics.md`. Key findings include:

1. **Progression Systems**  
   - **Meta-progression**: Retain stats (score, speed) between runs for cumulative growth.  
   - **Level-based progression**: Escalate difficulty (faster speed, more enemies) as levels increase.  
   - **Wave progression**: Increase enemy count/hardness in successive waves.  
   - **Permadeath**: Die once per run, but retain stats for future runs.  

2. **Power-ups (7 examples)**  
   - Speed boost, shield, multi-score, time slowdown, fire breath, teleport, invincibility.  
   - These mechanics add strategic depth and vary gameplay dynamics.  

3. **Enemy Types**  
   - Stationary hazards (e.g., lava tiles), patrolling enemies, chasing enemies, and eatable enemies (consumed for points/health).  
   - Enemies interact differently with the snake (collision = death vs. "defeating" them).  

4. **Roguelike Loop**  
   - Procedural generation, permadeath, randomized loot, and progression through increasingly difficult levels.  
   - Key references: *Snakelike* (permadeath + procedural generation), *Rogue Snake* (level progression + combat), and *roguelike-snake* (random augments).  

The document now serves as a foundation for implementing these mechanics into a snake game. Let me know if you'd like to expand on any section!

messages.content:
Build a complete, self-contained, playable browser game: a roguelike snake game.

Core requirements:
- The player controls a snake that explores procedurally generated maps - not a single fixed
  layout. Each run/level should be generated with a real procedural-generation technique, with
  meaningful variety across playthroughs.
- The snake collects powerups scattered through the map that grant meaningful gameplay effects
  (your design choice of a good, coherent set - e.g. speed changes, temporary invincibility,
  score multipliers, size effects, etc).
- There are enemies on the map the snake must avoid or defeat - define clear, fair rules for how
  the snake defeats or avoids them, and what happens on failure.
- Real roguelike structure appropriate for a snake game: meaningful run-to-run progression and/or
  permadeath and/or escalating difficulty - use your own design judgment, but make it a genuine
  roguelike, not just "snake with extra sprites."
- Must be playable by opening a single HTML file directly in a browser: no build step, no
  external network dependencies at runtime (bundle or hand-write everything you need).
- Write clean, correct, working code. You have no way to visually test the game yourself, so
  compensate by re-reading what you wrote, checking logic carefully, and reasoning through edge
  cases before considering something done.

Process: research real roguelike design and procedural map generation techniques suited to a
snake game (use your web_search/web_fetch tools - don't guess at a technique if you're unsure,
look it up) before you plan. Then plan the architecture and gameplay systems, implement it fully,
and critique + iterate on your own work before calling it finished. Use your complete research ->
plan -> critique -> implement -> iterate process - this is exactly the kind of hard, open-ended
project you're built to spend real effort on across many turns. Do not ask me questions; make
reasonable design decisions yourself and state them in your final report.

When - and only when - you believe the game is genuinely complete and playable, stop calling
tools and give a final report: what you built, how the procedural generation works, what
powerups and enemies exist and how they behave, and exactly which file to open to play it.

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-1 report]
**Research Findings: Procedural Generation Techniques for Snake Game**

1. **Cellular Automata (CA)**  
   - **Pros**: Generates organic, cave-like structures with natural patterns. Effective for creating "pockets" and irregular terrain.  
   - **Cons**: Requires post-processing to refine maps (e.g., removing isolated areas). May produce overly chaotic layouts.  
   - **Relevance**: Suitable for snake game maps with organic, navigable caves.  

2. **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP)**  
   - **Pros**: Creates structured, room-based layouts with predictable room sizes and corridors. Easy to implement for organized maps.  
   - **Cons**: Lacks organic feel; may result in repetitive or rigid layouts.  
   - **Relevance**: Ideal for snake games requiring distinct, intentional room layouts.  

3. **Drunkard's Walk (DW)**  
   - **Pros**: Produces natural, winding paths and fully connected maps. Great for organic cave systems.  
   - **Cons**: Limited to paths; struggles with creating large, open areas.  
   - **Relevance**: Excellent for snake game paths and corridors, ensuring navigability.  

4. **Random Walk/Noise-based Generation**  
   - **Pros**: Noise-based methods (e.g., Perlin noise) allow for varied terrain. Random walk ensures connectivity.  
   - **Cons**: Complex to implement; noise-based methods may lack control over specific features.  
   - **Relevance**: Useful for adding variation, but better combined with other techniques for structured maps.  

**Recommendation**:  
- **Combine BSP and DW**: Use BSP to create structured rooms and DW to generate organic corridors between them. This balances intentional layouts with natural pathways.  
- **Alternative**: Use Cellular Automata with post-processing to refine cave-like structures, ensuring navigable "pockets" and corridors.  

**Summary**:  
- **BSP** is best for structured, room-based maps.  
- **DW** excels at creating organic, connected paths.  
- A hybrid approach (BSP + DW) or CA with

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-2 report]
The research on roguelike mechanics for a snake game has been completed and saved to `research_roguelike_mechanics.md`. Key findings include:

1. **Progression Systems**  
   - **Meta-progression**: Retain stats (score, speed) between runs for cumulative growth.  
   - **Level-based progression**: Escalate difficulty (faster speed, more enemies) as levels increase.  
   - **Wave progression**: Increase enemy count/hardness in successive waves.  
   - **Permadeath**: Die once per run, but retain stats for future runs.  

2. **Power-ups (7 examples)**  
   - Speed boost, shield, multi-score, time slowdown, fire breath, teleport, invincibility.  
   - These mechanics add strategic depth and vary gameplay dynamics.  

3. **Enemy Types**  
   - Stationary hazards (e.g., lava tiles), patrolling enemies, chasing enemies, and eatable enemies (consumed for points/health).  
   - Enemies interact differently with the snake (collision = death vs. "defeating" them).  

4. **Roguelike Loop**  
   - Procedural generation, permadeath, randomized loot, and progression through increasingly difficult levels.  
   - Key references: *Snakelike* (permadeath + procedural generation), *Rogue Snake* (level progression + combat), and *roguelike-snake* (random augments).  

The document now serves as a foundation for implementing these mechanics into a snake game. Let me know if you'd like to expand on any section!

messages.content:
[helper-3 report]
The implementation plan for the Snake Roguelike game has been created and saved in `plan.md`. The plan includes the following sections:

1. **Technical Architecture**: The game will be structured as a single HTML file with sections for HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. The JavaScript will be modular, with separate functions for map generation, game state management, entity management, and collision detection.

2. **Map Generation**: The map will be generated using the Drunkard's Walk algorithm, which randomly places rooms and corridors to create a natural, maze-like structure.

3. **Game State & Loop**: The game will have three main states: Menu, Playing, and GameOver. The game loop will run continuously, updating the game state and rendering the canvas at a fixed interval.

4. **Snake Mechanics**: The snake will move in four directions based on user input. The snake will grow in length when it eats food, and the game will check for collisions with walls, enemies, and itself.

5. **Power-up System**: The game will include several power-ups, including Speed Boost, Shield, Multi-Score, Time Slowdown, Fire Breath, Teleport, and Invincibility.

6. **Enemy System**: The game will include several enemy types, including Stationary Hazards, Patrolling Enemies, Chasing Enemies, and Eatable Enemies.

7. **Progression System**: The game will escalate difficulty as the player progresses through levels. Each level will have faster snake speed, more enemies, and new power-ups and enemy types. The player will need to survive multiple waves to advance to the next level.

8. **Implementation Steps**: The implementation steps include setting up the project, implementing the game canvas, implementing map generation, implementing game states, implementing entity management, implementing snake mechanics, implementing the power-up system, implementing the enemy system, implementing the progression system, and testing and refining the game.

The plan is detailed enough for a de

3. **Technical Constraints**: Does the plan ensure the game is fully self-contained in one HTML file (no external assets, no external libraries)?
4. **Game Loop**: Is the "fixed interval" rendering mentioned in the plan sufficient for a smooth experience? (e.g., using `requestAnimationFrame`).
5. **Edge Cases**: Are there any missing edge cases (e.g., snake getting stuck in a 1-tile wide corridor, handling rapid input, etc.)?

Write the critique to `critique.md`."}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-4 report]
## Critique of Snake Roguelike Game Plan

### 1. Feasibility of Drunkard's Walk Algorithm  
- **Concern**: The algorithm risks creating non-navigable maps (e.g., blobby areas or 1-tile-wide corridors). The plan lacks mechanisms to enforce minimum corridor widths or prevent dead-ends, which could trap the snake or make the map feel chaotic.  

### 2. Scope of Power-Ups & Enemies  
- **Concern**: 7 power-ups and 4 enemy types may overwhelm a single-file implementation. While modular JS can handle this, it risks bloating code and complicating collision/behavior logic. Prioritizing core mechanics (snake movement, basic enemies) before adding complexity is advisable.  

### 3. Technical Constraints  
- **Verdict**: The plan is feasible for a single HTML file (no external assets/libs). However, complex features like animations or physics may require workarounds (e.g., canvas optimizations) due to JS limitations.  

### 4. Game Loop & Rendering  
- **Concern**: Fixed-interval rendering may cause inconsistent frame rates. The plan should adopt `requestAnimationFrame` for smoother, more responsive gameplay, especially for fast-paced snake movement and enemy AI.  

### 5. Missing Edge Cases  
- **Issues**:  
  - No handling for 1-tile-wide corridors (snake could get stuck).  
  - No input debouncing for rapid key presses (e.g., diagonal movement conflicts).  
  - No documentation for power-up interaction rules (e.g., overlapping effects like "Speed Boost + Teleport").  

**Recommendations**: Add map validation for navigability, simplify power-up/enemy counts initially, use `requestAnimationFrame`, and document input/debounce logic.

function.name: spawn_agent
function.arguments:
{"model":"victory4-8b","task":"Revise the plan in `plan.md` based on the critique in `critique.md`.

Key revisions to incorporate:
1. **Map Generation**: Refine the Drunkard's Walk or add a post-processing step to ensure corridors are at least 2 tiles wide and the map is fully navigable.
2. **Scope Management**: Prioritize a core set of 4-5 power-ups and 3 enemy types to ensure a polished experience in a single file.
3. **Game Loop**: Replace "fixed interval" with `requestAnimationFrame` for smoother rendering.
4. **Input Handling**: Add logic for input debouncing/buffering to prevent the snake from turning into itself on rapid key presses.
5. **Edge Cases**: Explicitly include logic for handling narrow corridors and clear interaction rules for power-ups.

Update `plan.md` with these improvements."}

File paths are relative to the project root, which IS this project's
folder - e.g. use "game.js" and "index.html" directly, NOT the project folder's own name as a
prefix. If any file operation fails, call list_files with path="." FIRST to see what is
actually there, then proceed - do not ask a question and stop, there is no one to answer it.

Create game.js from scratch containing a MINIMAL, fully working, correct classic
snake game: canvas rendering on a grid, the snake as an array of segments, arrow-key/WASD
input changing direction (never allowing an immediate 180-degree reverse into itself), a game
loop (setInterval or requestAnimationFrame with timing) that moves the snake each tick, a
single food item that respawns at a random empty cell when eaten (snake grows by one segment),
collision detection that ends the game on hitting a wall or itself, and a score counter updated
in the DOM matching index.html's elements. Do NOT add procedural maps, powerups, or enemies yet
- just a solid, correct, classic snake game. Write the COMPLETE file with write_file (not a
sketch or partial version).

messages.content:
## Implementation Plan for Snake Roguelike Game

### 1. Technical Architecture

The game will be structured as a single HTML file with three main sections:
- **HTML**: Contains the game canvas and UI elements (e.g., score, level, game state indicators).
- **CSS**: Styles the game canvas, UI, and animations for a clean, responsive design.
- **JavaScript**: Implements the game logic, including map generation, game loop, entity management, and power-up/enemy systems.

The JavaScript will be modular, with separate functions for:
- Map generation and rendering.
- Game state management (Menu, Playing, GameOver).
- Entity management (snake, enemies, power-ups).
- Collision detection and game mechanics (movement, growth, scoring).

### 2. Map Generation

**Method**: Drunkard's Walk with Post-Processing

**Algorithm**: The map will be generated using the Drunkard's Walk algorithm, which randomly places rooms and corridors. This method ensures a natural, maze-like structure with varied paths and hidden areas. The algorithm will:
1. Start with a single room.
2. Randomly select a direction to expand the map.
3. Continue expanding until the map reaches the desired size.
4. Fill in the remaining spaces with walls or obstacles.

**Post-Processing**: After generating the map, a post-processing step will ensure that all corridors are at least 2 tiles wide and that the map is fully navigable. This step will also remove any dead-end corridors or narrow passages that could make the map non-navigable.

### 3. Game State & Loop

**Game Loop**: The game will run in a continuous loop, updating the game state and rendering the canvas using `requestAnimationFrame` for smoother rendering.

**Game States**: The game will have three main states:
- **Menu**: Displays the start screen and options (e.g., New Game, Load Game, Quit).
- **Playing**: The main game state where the player controls the snake, collects power-ups, and avoids enemies.
- **GameOver**: Displays the game over screen and option

function.arguments: {"model":"qwen3-4b-stock","task":"Research procedural generation techniques suitable for a 2D grid-based game (like Snake). Specifically, explain Cellular Automata, Binary Space Partitioning (BSP), and Drunkard's Walk. Provide a brief overview of how each could be used to create \"rooms\" or \"caves\" in a roguelike context. Output the findings as a concise summary for a developer."}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-1 report]
**Procedural Generation Techniques for Roguelikes:**

1. **Cellular Automata (CA):**  
   - Uses a grid of cells that evolve based on rules (e.g., alive/dead states).  
   - In roguelikes, a grid is initialized with random cell states, then iteratively updated to create smooth, organic chambers and passages.  
   - Ideal for generating natural, interconnected cave systems with smooth transitions.

2. **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP):**  
   - Recursively divides space into rooms and corridors by splitting the grid with planes.  
   - Creates structured, hierarchical layouts with defined room sizes and connectivity.  
   - Best for producing clean, organized rooms with deliberate corridor paths.

3. **Drunkard's Walk (Random Walk):**  
   - Simulates a random path by moving in random directions, leaving trails that form cave-like structures.  
   - Generates organic, sprawling layouts with interconnected passages, ideal for large, open cave systems.  
   - Often used to fill in gaps or connect BSP-generated rooms.

**Combined Use in Roguelikes:**  
Hybrid approaches (e.g., BSP + CA + Drunkard's Walk) are common. For example:  
- **CA** creates smooth chambers,  
- **BSP** ensures structured rooms,  
- **Drunkard's Walk** adds organic connectivity.  
This combination balances coherence and randomness, avoiding pathological layouts while maintaining gameplay diversity.

1. Procedural level generation (how it affects snake movement/pathing).
2. Power-ups (e.g., speed boost, size reduction, temporary invulnerability).
3. Enemies or hazards (moving obstacles, traps, items that change state).
4. Progression systems (levels, experience points, permanent upgrades between "runs").
5. Environmental effects (fog of war, limited visibility, changing terrain types).

Provide a list of 5-7 specific mechanics that would be most impactful and feasible for a web-based snake game."}

messages.content:
## Implementation Plan for Snake Roguelike Game

### 1. Technical Architecture

The game will be structured as a single HTML file with three main sections:
- **HTML**: Contains the game canvas and UI elements (e.g., score, level, game state indicators).
- **CSS**: Styles the game canvas, UI, and animations for a clean, responsive design.
- **JavaScript**: Implements the game logic, including map generation, game loop, entity management, and power-up/enemy systems.

The JavaScript will be modular, with separate functions for:
- Map generation and rendering.
- Game state management (Menu, Playing, GameOver).
- Entity management (snake, enemies, power-ups).
- Collision detection and game mechanics (movement, growth, scoring).

### 2. Map Generation

**Method**: Drunkard's Walk with Post-Processing

**Algorithm**: The map will be generated using the Drunkard's Walk algorithm, which randomly places rooms and corridors. This method ensures a natural, maze-like structure with varied paths and hidden areas. The algorithm will:
1. Start with a single room.
2. Randomly select a direction to expand the map.
3. Continue expanding until the map reaches the desired size.
4. Fill in the remaining spaces with walls or obstacles.

**Post-Processing**: After generating the map, a post-processing step will ensure that all corridors are at least 2 tiles wide and that the map is fully navigable. This step will also remove any dead-end corridors or narrow passages that could make the map non-navigable.

### 3. Game State & Loop

**Game Loop**: The game will run in a continuous loop, updating the game state and rendering the canvas using `requestAnimationFrame` for smoother rendering.

**Game States**: The game will have three main states:
- **Menu**: Displays the start screen and options (e.g., New Game, Load Game, Quit).
- **Playing**: The main game state where the player controls the snake, collects power-ups, and avoids enemies.
- **GameOver**: Displays the game over screen and option

Specifically, explain Cellular Automata, Binary Space Partitioning (BSP), and Drunkard's Walk. Provide a brief overview of how each could be used to create \"rooms\" or \"caves\" in a roguelike context. Output the findings as a concise summary for a developer."}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-1 report]
**Procedural Generation Techniques for Roguelikes:**

1. **Cellular Automata**  
   - Uses a grid of cells that evolve based on rules (e.g., alive/dead states). Over time, this creates organic, maze-like patterns.  
   - **Roguelike Use**: Generates smooth chambers and cave systems by iteratively modifying cell states, resulting in natural, interconnected spaces.

2. **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP)**  
   - Recursively splits the game world into rooms and corridors by dividing space with planes or walls.  
   - **Roguelike Use**: Creates structured, hierarchical layouts with defined rooms and pathways, ideal for deliberate room placement.

3. **Drunkard's Walk**  
   - Simulates random movement (like a drunk person wandering) to carve tunnels and corridors.  
   - **Roguelike Use**: Produces organic, sprawling cave systems with interconnected paths, adding unpredictability to dungeon layouts.

**Combined Approach**:  
Many roguelikes blend these techniques (e.g., BSP for structure, Cellular Automata for caves, Drunkard's Walk for tunnels) to balance order and randomness, ensuring coherent, varied levels. Each method’s strengths complement others, avoiding pathological layouts while maintaining procedural diversity.

3. Obstacles/Enemies (e.g., moving walls, enemies that chase the snake, "poison" food).
4. Environmental hazards (e.g., shrinking zones, shifting walls).

Provide a concise list of mechanics that could transform basic Snake into a roguelike experience."}

function.arguments: {"model":"qwen3-4b-stock","task":"Research procedural generation techniques suitable for a 2D grid-based game (like Snake). Specifically, explain Cellular Automata, Binary Space Partitioning (BSP), and Drunkard's Walk. Provide a brief overview of how each could be used to create \"rooms\" or \"caves\" in a roguelike context. Output the findings as a concise summary for a developer."}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-1 report]
**Procedural Generation Techniques for Roguelikes:**

1. **Cellular Automata (CA):**  
   - Uses a grid of cells that evolve based on rules (e.g., alive/dead states).  
   - In roguelikes, a grid is initialized with random cell states, then iteratively updated to create smooth, organic chambers and passages.  
   - Ideal for generating natural, interconnected cave systems with smooth transitions.

2. **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP):**  
   - Recursively divides space into rooms and corridors by splitting the grid with planes.  
   - Creates structured, hierarchical layouts with defined room sizes and connectivity.  
   - Best for producing clean, organized rooms with deliberate corridor paths.

3. **Drunkard's Walk (Random Walk):**  
   - Simulates a random path by moving in random directions, leaving trails that form cave-like structures.  
   - Generates organic, sprawling layouts with interconnected passages, ideal for large, open cave systems.  
   - Often used to fill in gaps or connect BSP-generated rooms.

**Combined Use in Roguelikes:**  
Hybrid approaches (e.g., BSP + CA + Drunkard's Walk) are common. For example:  
- **CA** creates smooth chambers,  
- **BSP** ensures structured rooms,  
- **Drunkard's Walk** adds organic connectivity.  
This combination balances coherence and randomness, avoiding pathological layouts while maintaining gameplay diversity.

- **JavaScript**: Implements the game logic, including map generation, game loop, entity management, and power-up/enemy systems.

The JavaScript will be modular, with separate functions for:
- Map generation and rendering.
- Game state management (Menu, Playing, GameOver).
- Entity management (snake, enemies, power-ups).
- Collision detection and game mechanics (movement, growth, scoring).

### 2. Map Generation

**Method**: Drunkard's Walk with Post-Processing

**Algorithm**: The map will be generated using the Drunkard's Walk algorithm, which randomly places rooms and corridors. This method ensures a natural, maze-like structure with varied paths and hidden areas. The algorithm will:
1. Start with a single room.
2. Randomly select a direction to expand the map.
3. Continue expanding until the map reaches the desired size.
4. Fill in the remaining spaces with walls or obstacles.

**Post-Processing**: After generating the map, a post-processing step will ensure that all corridors are at least 2 tiles wide and that the map is fully navigable. This step will also remove any dead-end corridors or narrow passages that could make the map non-navigable.

### 3. Game State & Loop

**Game Loop**: The game will run in a continuous loop, updating the game state and rendering the canvas using `requestAnimationFrame` for smoother rendering.

**Game States**: The game will have three main states:
- **Menu**: Displays the start screen and options (e.g., New Game, Load Game, Quit).
- **Playing**: The main game state where the player controls the snake, collects power-ups, and avoids enemies.
- **GameOver**: Displays the game over screen and option

function.arguments: {"model":"qwen3-4b-stock","task":"Research procedural generation techniques suitable for a 2D grid-based game (like Snake). Specifically, explain Cellular Automata, Binary Space Partitioning (BSP), and Drunkard's Walk. Provide a brief overview of how each could be used to create \"rooms\" or \"caves\" in a roguelike context. Output the findings as a concise summary for a developer."}

messages.name: spawn_agent
messages.content:
[helper-1 report]
**Procedural Generation Techniques for Roguelikes:**

1. **Cellular Automata (CA):**  
   - Uses a grid of cells that evolve based on rules (e.g., alive/dead states).  
   - In roguelikes, a grid is initialized with random cell states, then iteratively updated to create smooth, organic chambers and passages.  
   - Ideal for generating natural, interconnected cave systems with smooth transitions.

2. **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP):**  
   - Recursively divides space into rooms and corridors by splitting the grid with planes.  
   - Creates structured, hierarchical layouts with defined room sizes and connectivity.  
   - Best for producing clean, organized rooms with deliberate corridor paths.

3. **Drunkard's Walk (Random Walk):**  
   - Simulates a random path by moving in random directions, leaving trails that form cave-like structures.  
   - Generates organic, sprawling layouts with interconnected passages, ideal for large, open cave systems.  
   - Often used to fill in gaps or connect BSP-generated rooms.

**Combined Use in Roguelikes:**  
Hybrid approaches (e.g., BSP + CA + Drunkard's Walk) are common. For example:  
- **CA** creates smooth chambers,  
- **BSP** ensures structured rooms,  
- **Drunkard's Walk** adds organic connectivity.  
This combination balances coherence and randomness, avoiding pathological layouts while maintaining gameplay diversity.

3. Enemies or hazards (moving obstacles, traps, items that change state).
4. Progression systems (levels, experience points, permanent upgrades between "runs").
5. Environmental effects (fog of war, limited visibility, changing terrain types).

Provide a list of 5-7 specific mechanics that would be most impactful and feasible for a web-based snake game."}
